name: Drama
description: Character-driven stories that emphasize emotional stakes, interpersonal conflict, and consequential choices, often grounded in plausible human experience. (This graph has 18 nodes — one more than the 17-node standard — because it includes a second Anti-Pattern node to capture an additional genre-specific pitfall.)

nodes.label: Drama Promise
nodes.definition: The audience is promised emotionally consequential stories where characters face meaningful choices under pressure, and the outcome matters because the stakes are grounded in recognizable human experience.
exit_conditions: Core constraints are established that fulfill this promise
failure_modes: Promise of emotional weight but delivering melodrama without earned consequences
Claiming realism while relying on contrivance
signals_in_text: Tone is grounded and serious
Characters face situations with real emotional cost
Outcomes feel consequential rather than arbitrary

nodes.label: Emotional Stakes Must Be Real
nodes.definition: The central conflict must carry genuine emotional weight â€” characters must have something to lose that the audience can feel.
exit_conditions: Stakes are defined in terms of relationships, identity, or values the character holds dear
failure_modes: Stakes are abstract or theoretical rather than felt
Audience cannot identify what the character stands to lose
signals_in_text: Early scenes establish what the character values most
Threats are directed at those valued things

nodes.label: Characters Must Make Consequential Choices
nodes.definition: The story must hinge on decisions the characters make, not merely on things that happen to them. Choices must have irreversible consequences.
exit_conditions: At least one major choice with visible consequences drives the plot
typical_variants: Choice that sacrifices one relationship for another
failure_modes: Characters are purely reactive â€” things happen to them but they never decide
Choices have no visible consequences
signals_in_text: Scenes built around decision moments
Aftermath scenes showing consequences of choices

nodes.label: Grounded in Plausible Human Experience
nodes.definition: The story must feel psychologically and emotionally plausible â€” characters behave in ways consistent with recognizable human motivation, even in extraordinary circumstances.
exit_conditions: Character motivations are legible and internally consistent
failure_modes: Characters act out of plot convenience rather than motivation
Emotional responses are disproportionate or unmotivated
signals_in_text: Behavior tracks to established character psychology
Social context influences character choices realistically

nodes.label: Conflict Is Primarily Interpersonal
nodes.definition: The central tension arises from relationships between characters â€” disagreements, betrayals, competing needs, or failures of understanding â€” rather than from external spectacle.
exit_conditions: At least one relationship is under meaningful pressure throughout the story
failure_modes: Conflict is purely external with no interpersonal dimension
Relationships are static and unaffected by events
signals_in_text: Key scenes are conversations or confrontations between characters
Tension is visible in what characters say and don't say to each other

nodes.label: Domestic Drama
nodes.definition: Drama set within family or household structures, where intimate relationships carry the primary emotional weight.
entry_conditions: Setting is primarily domestic or familial
exit_conditions: Family relationships are tested and transformed
failure_modes: Family conflict feels trivial or petty rather than consequential
Resolution is sentimental rather than earned
signals_in_text: Scenes set in homes, family gatherings, private spaces
Dialogue reveals family history and unspoken tensions

nodes.label: Social Drama
nodes.definition: Drama where systemic forces â€” class, race, institutions, economic pressure â€” create the conditions for interpersonal conflict.
entry_conditions: Social or institutional systems are visible forces in the story
exit_conditions: Characters navigate or confront systemic pressure with personal consequences
failure_modes: System is a backdrop rather than an active force
Reduces to sermon without human story
signals_in_text: Characters reference or struggle with institutional rules
Power dynamics are visible in who speaks and who is silenced

nodes.label: Courtroom Drama
nodes.definition: Drama structured around legal proceedings, where justice, truth, and institutional process provide the framework for interpersonal conflict.
entry_conditions: Legal system is the primary arena
exit_conditions: A verdict or legal outcome carries emotional and moral weight
failure_modes: Legal procedure overwhelms the human story
Verdict feels arbitrary rather than earned
signals_in_text: Courtroom scenes as set pieces
Evidence and testimony reveal character rather than just facts

nodes.label: Tragedy-Inflected Drama
nodes.definition: Drama where the arc bends toward loss or destruction, driven by character flaws or irreconcilable circumstances.
entry_conditions: Central character has a flaw or faces impossible circumstances
exit_conditions: Loss or destruction is the consequence of character and choice, not random misfortune
failure_modes: Suffering is arbitrary rather than connected to character
Downfall is inevitable from the start, removing dramatic tension
signals_in_text: Early hints of the flaw or impossible circumstance
Moments where a different choice could have changed the outcome

nodes.label: Confined or Intimate Setting
nodes.definition: Drama tends toward settings that concentrate interpersonal pressure â€” small spaces, closed communities, institutions â€” rather than sprawling geographies.
exit_conditions: Setting physically and socially constrains characters, forcing interaction
failure_modes: Setting is so open that characters can simply walk away from conflict
Physical environment has no bearing on emotional dynamics
signals_in_text: Repeated locations
Characters cannot easily escape each other
Physical proximity drives confrontation

nodes.label: Time Compression or Deadline
nodes.definition: Drama often compresses time to intensify stakes â€” a weekend, a trial period, a final visit, a deadline â€” forcing decisions that might otherwise be deferred.
exit_conditions: Time constraint prevents characters from avoiding difficult choices
typical_variants: Ritual or event deadline (wedding, funeral, holiday)
failure_modes: Time pressure feels arbitrary
Deadline is introduced but has no effect on pacing or choices
signals_in_text: References to time remaining
Characters aware they must act now
Pacing accelerates as deadline approaches

nodes.label: Social Rules Constrain Behavior
nodes.definition: The story's social context imposes rules â€” spoken or unspoken â€” that characters must navigate, resist, or break at personal cost.
exit_conditions: At least one character is constrained by social expectations that conflict with their needs
failure_modes: Social rules are stated but never enforced
Characters break rules without consequence
signals_in_text: Characters modify behavior based on who is watching
Private vs. public selves diverge
Punishment or ostracism for rule-breaking

nodes.label: Confrontation Scene
nodes.definition: At least one scene where characters directly face the central interpersonal conflict â€” not through action but through dialogue, accusation, confession, or silence.
exit_conditions: Characters have said (or pointedly not said) what the conflict requires
failure_modes: Confrontation is deflected or interrupted before it lands
Characters say exactly what they mean with no subtext
signals_in_text: Long dialogue scene with high emotional charge
Subtext carries more weight than surface words
Physical staging reflects power dynamics

nodes.label: Irreversible Choice Scene
nodes.definition: A scene where the protagonist makes a decision that cannot be undone â€” choosing one relationship over another, telling or withholding a truth, staying or leaving.
exit_conditions: Choice is made and consequences begin
failure_modes: Choice is reversed shortly after with no cost
Character is forced rather than choosing
Consequences are delayed indefinitely
signals_in_text: Character visibly weighs options
Scene ends with a door closed â€” literally or figuratively

nodes.label: Aftermath / New Normal Scene
nodes.definition: A scene showing the world after the central conflict has resolved â€” demonstrating that choices had lasting consequences and the characters are changed.
exit_conditions: Audience can see the permanent impact of the story's events
failure_modes: Story ends at the climax with no aftermath
Aftermath contradicts the weight of what happened
signals_in_text: Tone shifts to reflective
Daily life details show what has changed
Echoes of earlier scenes with altered meaning

nodes.label: Sustained Serious Tone
nodes.definition: Drama requires a predominant tone of seriousness â€” events are treated as consequential, emotions are not played for laughs, and the narrative voice respects the gravity of what characters experience.
exit_conditions: Tone is maintained through scene construction, dialogue register, and pacing
failure_modes: Tone shifts to comedy or spectacle without earning the shift
Seriousness becomes pretentiousness without emotional grounding
signals_in_text: Prose or cinematography favors stillness over spectacle
Humor, if present, is character-driven and situational rather than gag-based

nodes.label: Unearned Emotional Manipulation
nodes.definition: Drama fails when emotional responses are manufactured through contrivance â€” convenient deaths, coincidences, or suffering without narrative logic â€” rather than earned through character and choice.
entry_conditions: Emotional moments occur in the story
exit_conditions: Emotional beats are traceable to established character psychology and prior events
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode â€” its presence indicates a genre violation
signals_in_text: Emotional peaks feel unconnected to prior scenes
Audience is told to feel rather than shown why

nodes.label: Purely Passive Protagonist
nodes.definition: Drama fails when the central character never makes a meaningful choice â€” when things happen to them but they never act with agency.
exit_conditions: Protagonist makes at least one consequential choice that drives the plot
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode â€” its presence indicates a genre violation
signals_in_text: Key turning points happen without protagonist input
Other characters make all the decisions

edges.label: Promise requires real stakes
edges.effects_on_stakes: Defines that stakes must be emotional and felt, not abstract
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must have something valuable at risk
common_alternatives: Some dramas foreground moral stakes over emotional stakes
anti_patterns: Stakes announced but never tested

edges.label: Promise requires consequential choices
edges.effects_on_stakes: Ensures outcomes depend on character decisions
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must be agents, not passengers
common_alternatives: Ensemble dramas may distribute agency across multiple characters
anti_patterns: Plot resolved by coincidence or external force

edges.label: Promise requires plausibility
edges.effects_on_stakes: Grounds stakes in recognizable human psychology
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must behave consistently with established motivation
common_alternatives: Allegorical drama may stretch plausibility while maintaining emotional truth
anti_patterns: Characters act as mouthpieces for theme rather than as people

edges.label: Promise requires interpersonal conflict
edges.effects_on_stakes: Centers the conflict in relationships
edges.effects_on_character: Characters are defined by how they relate to each other
common_alternatives: Some dramas include external conflict but always refract it through relationships
anti_patterns: Conflict is purely external (disaster, war) with no interpersonal dimension

edges.label: Promise requires serious tone
edges.effects_on_stakes: Tone signals that events carry weight
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' experiences are treated with gravity
common_alternatives: Dramedy blends serious and comic tones but maintains dramatic weight in key scenes
anti_patterns: Serious subject matter treated flippantly throughout

edges.label: Emotional stakes in family context
preconditions: Emotional stakes are rooted in family bonds
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes become specifically familial â€” love, loyalty, inheritance, belonging
edges.effects_on_character: Characters defined by family roles and history
common_alternatives: Social drama
Courtroom drama
anti_patterns: Family setting present but family relationships irrelevant to conflict

edges.label: Emotional stakes in social context
preconditions: Emotional stakes are shaped by systemic forces
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes include systemic injustice, class pressure, or institutional power
edges.effects_on_character: Characters navigate personal needs against social structures
common_alternatives: Domestic drama
Courtroom drama
anti_patterns: Social setting present but system has no effect on characters

edges.label: Emotional stakes in legal context
preconditions: Emotional stakes are channeled through legal proceedings
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes include justice, freedom, and public judgment
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must perform within legal constraints while pursuing personal truth
common_alternatives: Social drama
Domestic drama
anti_patterns: Legal procedure present but verdict has no emotional weight

edges.label: Emotional stakes toward tragic arc
preconditions: Emotional stakes are tied to character flaw or impossible circumstance
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes escalate toward loss that feels inevitable but not predetermined
edges.effects_on_character: Character's flaw or situation makes a positive outcome progressively less likely
common_alternatives: Domestic drama
Social drama
anti_patterns: Tragedy is random rather than connected to character

edges.label: Domestic drama concentrates in intimate spaces
edges.effects_on_stakes: Physical proximity prevents avoidance of conflict
edges.effects_on_character: Characters cannot escape each other
common_alternatives: Some domestic dramas span multiple locations but maintain emotional confinement
anti_patterns: Characters in a domestic story never share physical space

edges.label: Social drama requires visible social rules
edges.effects_on_stakes: Social rules create additional constraints on character behavior
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must navigate between personal needs and social expectations
common_alternatives: Rules may be formal (laws, codes) or informal (norms, expectations)
anti_patterns: Social system is referenced but never constrains any character

edges.label: Courtroom concentrates action in single space
edges.effects_on_stakes: Courtroom as arena intensifies confrontation
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must perform under public scrutiny
common_alternatives: Some courtroom dramas alternate between courtroom and investigation scenes
anti_patterns: Courtroom drama that never shows the courtroom

edges.label: Trial imposes deadline
edges.effects_on_stakes: Trial schedule creates urgency
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must prepare and act within legal timelines
common_alternatives: Deadline may be verdict date, sentencing, or statute of limitations
anti_patterns: Trial has no time pressure â€” proceedings feel endless

edges.label: Tragic arc compresses toward inevitable end
edges.effects_on_stakes: Time running out intensifies the sense of approaching loss
edges.effects_on_character: Character's window for redemption or change narrows
common_alternatives: Time pressure may be explicit (illness) or implicit (escalating consequences)
anti_patterns: Tragic arc with no sense of approaching reckoning

edges.label: Confinement forces confrontation
preconditions: Characters are confined together with unresolved tension
edges.effects_on_stakes: Avoidance becomes impossible
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must face each other directly
common_alternatives: Confrontation may be verbal, physical, or through pointed silence
anti_patterns: Characters confined together but conflict never surfaces

edges.label: Deadline forces irreversible choice
edges.effects_on_stakes: Choice cannot be deferred â€” act now or lose the chance
edges.effects_on_character: Character must commit under pressure
common_alternatives: Choice may be proactive or a refusal to act that itself has consequences
anti_patterns: Deadline passes with no decision required

edges.label: Social rules create impossible choice
preconditions: Social rules conflict with character's needs
edges.effects_on_stakes: Breaking rules carries social cost; obeying them carries personal cost
edges.effects_on_character: Character must choose between conformity and authenticity
common_alternatives: Choice may be public defiance or private compromise
anti_patterns: Social rules present but character faces no difficult choice about them

edges.label: Confrontation leads to new reality
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes are resolved or permanently altered
edges.effects_on_character: Characters live with the consequences of what was said and done
common_alternatives: Aftermath may be reconciliation, separation, or uneasy truce
anti_patterns: Confrontation occurs but nothing changes

edges.label: Choice produces lasting consequences
edges.effects_on_stakes: Consequences of the choice become the new reality
edges.effects_on_character: Character must live with what they chose
common_alternatives: Consequences may be immediate or revealed gradually
anti_patterns: Choice is made but consequences are never shown

edges.label: Stakes without grounding become melodrama
preconditions: Emotional stakes are claimed but not earned
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes feel manufactured rather than organic
edges.effects_on_character: Characters become puppets of plot rather than agents
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern â€” it describes what to avoid

edges.label: Choices required â€” passivity violates genre
preconditions: Protagonist faces situations but never decides
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes lose meaning because no one is choosing
edges.effects_on_character: Character becomes an observer of their own story
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern â€” it describes what to avoid
